coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of single-component graphene quantum dots for stable and bright white luminescence films as a phosphor
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhi
Tan, Long
Hou, Pei-Pei
Jin, Xin-Jian
Li, Meng-Chao
Zhou, Qun-Yi
Liao, Peng
Zeng, Zheling
Deng, Shuguang
Dai, Gui-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the merits of low cost, non-toxicity and unique optical properties have attracted much attention in various fields. However, it is still challenging to synthesize one-component white-light emissive GQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). In this work, 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (1,5-DAN) and tartaric acid (TA) were smartly introduced as the precursors in a solvothermal reaction. As a matter of fact, the obtained GQDs exhibited a blue-emission band centered at 413 nm (surface state emission) and a yellow-green-emission band at 558 nm (core-state emission) might be originated from various surface functional groups and inner π-conjugated structure, respectively, which enabled white luminescence of the GQDs. It reveals that both of 1,5-DAN and TA played a vital role on forming the yellow-green-emission band by providing the components of benzene rings and CC/CO bonds, respectively. Moreover, the white-light emissive GQDs (W-GQDs) with the mean size of ∼2.7 nm exhibited a PLQY as high as 28.68% and good photostability under the 365 nm UV light and daylight. A PLQY up to 9.32% can still be achieved in the solid GQDs/polyvinyl alcohol film. Therefore, our prepared GQDs are promising for white light emitting diodes application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanostructures
Solvothermal method
Graphene quantum dots
Light emitting diodes
Quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of W-GQDs/epoxy resin film for luminescent letter pattern

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM images of W-GQDs at different magnifications. (d) HRTEM image of a single W-GQDs particle. (e) Corresponding SAED pattern of W-GQDs. (f) Diameter histogram of W-GQDs particles.
(a) AFM topography image of W-GQDs. (b) Corresponding height analysis of W-GQDs along the line in a. (c) Raman spectrum of W-GQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of W-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of W-GQDs. (b–d) High-resolution XPS (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s spectrum of W-GQDs. (e) Schematic diagram of the structure of W-GQDs.

(a) Absorption and (b) PL spectra of W-GQDs. The inset shows photographs of W-GQDs under ambient condition (left) and 365 nm UV light lamp, respectively. (c) Corresponding CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of W-GQDs at different excitation wavelength. (d) PL decay of W-GQDs at the emission wavelengths of 435 and 558 nm. (e–f) Photostability of W-GQDs solution under (e) daylight (purple) and (f) UV light (pink).

(a–b) Cameral pictures of W-GQDs/PVA film under (a) ambient illumination and (b) by 365 nm UV irradiation. (c) Flexibility test of W-GQDs/PVA film under the illumination of 365 nm UV lamp. (d–e) W-GQDs/epoxy resin composite with pattern of “NCU” letters under (d) ambient illumination and (e) UV light.
Schematic process of synthesizing W-GQDs by solvothermal treatment of 1,5-DAN and TA as organic precursors at 200 °C for fabricating white luminescence materials.
Schematic illustration of fabricating the W-GQDs/PVA film and W-GQDs/epoxy resin films.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of single-component graphene quantum dots for stable and bright white luminescence films as a phosphor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the merits of low cost, non-toxicity and unique optical properties have attracted much attention in various fields. However, it is still challenging to synthesize one-component white-light emissive GQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). In this work, 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (1,5-DAN) and tartaric acid (TA) were smartly introduced as the precursors in a solvothermal reaction. As a matter of fact, the obtained GQDs exhibited a blue-emission band centered at 413 nm (surface state emission) and a yellow-green-emission band at 558 nm (core-state emission) might be originated from various surface functional groups and inner π-conjugated structure, respectively, which enabled white luminescence of the GQDs. It reveals that both of 1,5-DAN and TA played a vital role on forming the yellow-green-emission band by providing the components of benzene rings and CC/CO bonds, respectively. Moreover, the white-light emissive GQDs (W-GQDs) with the mean size of ∼2.7 nm exhibited a PLQY as high as 28.68% and good photostability under the 365 nm UV light and daylight. A PLQY up to 9.32% can still be achieved in the solid GQDs/polyvinyl alcohol film. Therefore, our prepared GQDs are promising for white light emitting diodes application.

ce:section.ce:para: As a zero-dimensional carbon material with the size less than 10 nm, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted tremendous attention owing to their unique optical and electronic characteristics [1–3]. On this basis, GQDs have enormous potential for applications in the fields of fluorescence probe [4], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [5], photocatalysis [6], electrochemical energy storage [7] and electrocatalysis [8]. For GQDs synthesis, it can be mainly divided into two approaches of top-down and bottom-up method [9,10]. The top-down method refers to cut large carbon materials into small fragments to form GQDs, which can be completed by hydrothermal reaction, ultra-sonication, chemical oxidation and electrochemical exfoliation. Although the top-down method has the advantages of abundant raw materials and simple technique [11], the obtained GQDs normally have relative low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). For the bottom-up method, the synthesized GQDs by the carbonization of organic molecules in hydrothermal, solvothermal and microwave-assisted pyrolysis reaction normally exhibit better uniformity and higher PLQY than that of the top-down strategy [12], which make them promising in various applications.

To date, organic semiconductors and rare-earth quantum dots as the luminescent materials have been commonly applied in white light emitting diodes (WLED). However, they are quite limited for practical application because of the high cost and toxicity. Therefore, the GQDs with low-cost, outstanding fluorescence performance and good photostability emerged as alternative materials to solve the problems. For example, Mishra et al. prepared white light-emitting materials by mixing the orange emissive CdS:Cu quantum dots with blue-luminescent GQDs [13]. The pure GQDs-based WLED devices were also fabricated by mixing several types of GQDs with different emission colors. For instance, Ding et al. Fabricated the WLED device with a Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinate at (0.42, 0.41) by incorporating the GQDs with three components (blue, green, and red) in a certain ratio into the polymer films [14]. They were prepared by solvothermal route using different precursors, citric acid for blue GQDs, phthalic acid/o-phenylenediamine for green GQDs and p-phenylenediamine for the red one, respectively. Nevertheless, the preparation of different types of GQDs emitting various colors is complicated and time-consuming. Besides, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) among various types of GQDs could lead to the low luminous efficiency of WLED [15]. To overcome these drawbacks, Madhu et al. synthesized a type of single-component white-light-emitting GQDs by carbonization of poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) in a microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction [16]. However, the single-component white-light-emitting GQDs have a relatively low PLQY of 11.1%. Therefore, it is urgent to synthesize single-component white-light-emitting GQDs with high PLQY.

Herein, for the first time, we employed 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (1,5-DAN) and tartaric acid (TA) as the precursors to synthesize single-component white-light-emitting GQDs by a facile one-step solvothermal method. The retained nitrogen atoms from 1,5-DAN as the dopants in GQDs can benefit their PLQY and facilitate the formation of defects on the surface and in core states. Furthermore, the aromatic rings in 1,5-DAN molecules with abundant sp2-hybridized carbon domains lead to the core-state emission of W-GQDs (yellowish green light). Regarding to the TA molecules, they provided rich hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups would help the formation of more surface defects, resulting in the surface states emission (blue light). As a matter of fact, the as-prepared W-GQDs had a narrow size distribution and exhibited obvious excitation-independent photoluminescence. Importantly, the single-component white-light-emitting GQDs have a PLQY as high as 28.68%. These W-GQDs were employed as the luminescence material to fabricate W-GQDs/PVA film and W-GQDs/epoxy resin film, which can both emit bright white light. Therefore, we conclude that our prepared W-GQDs are promising for WLED application.

1,5-diaminonaphthalene (>97%), tartaric acid, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA; MW: 1500 g/mol), epoxy resin, curing agent-593, and ethanol were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water with an electrical resistivity of 17.9 MΩ was obtained by a Milli-Q system (Ulupure, Chengdu, China).

The preparation process of W-GQDs is displayed in Scheme 1 . In details, the 1, 5-diaminonaphthalene (20 mg) and tartaric acid (19 mg) in mole ratio of 1: 1 were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) under ultrasonic treatment of 5 min to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave (25 mL), which was heated at 200 °C for 10 h in an oven. The obtained GQDs solution was purified using microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to filter large particles and was further dialyzed to remove small molecules by using dialysis bag of 500 Da. The resulted W-GQDs product was stored at 4 °C for further characterization after lyophilization. For comparison, 1,5-diaminonaphthalene and tartaric acid (T-GQDs) were employed as single carbon source to obtain GQDs in the same conditions, denoted as D-GQDs and T-GQDs respectively.

PVA powder (1 g) was dissolved in DI water (20 mL) to form homogeneous solution by strongly stirring at 90 °C. After cooling to the room temperature, the W-GQDs (0.5 mg mL−1, 2 mL) was dissolved into 5 mL of PVA solution under agitation for 1 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was dropped into a glass petri dish as the mould. After drying under vacuum condition at 60 °C for 1 day, the flexible white luminescence film was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of W-GQDs/epoxy resin film for luminescent letter pattern
ce:section.ce:para: The mass ratio of epoxy resin and curing agent was 2:1 and then they were stirred continuously for 5 min in a beaker (20 mL). Similarly, the formed sticky fluid was mixed with W-GQDs (0.5 mg mL−1, 2 mL) by agitating with a glass rod. The white-emitting letter blocks of “NCU” were prepared ultimately by filling the colloid into a mould with grooves of letters and drying at room temperature after curing for 2 h in air.

To observe morphological characterizations and particle distribution of the GQDs, the diluted W-GQDs solution was drop into copper grids with supporting ultra-thin carbon membrane and the images of transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) were captured through a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at the voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific-ESCALAB 250 Xi with AI Kα radiation) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance with Cu Kα radiation) tests were conducted to analyze chemical state and element on the surface of the prepared sample. Raman spectrum were collected on a Horiba Evolution Raman spectroscopy system. The UV-absorption spectrum was measured using a Persee TU-1900 ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The PL fluorescence spectrum was acquired by a Hitachi FL-6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. An advanced fluorescence spectrophotometer of Edinburgh FLS-1000 equipped with absolute quantum yield (QY) measurement apparatus was performed to obtain the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of W-GQDs in ethanol solution by an integrating sphere calculating W-GQDs spectrum and pure solvent spectrum under excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) were recorded through a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer. The Agilent 5500 atomic force microscope (AFM) was operated to measure height distribution of W-GQDs.

Fig. 1 a–c shows the TEM image of the W-GQDs synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. It can be seen that W-GQDs have good monodispersity and sphere-like morphology. Representative lattice fringes (d = 0.24 nm) corresponding to the (100) facet of graphene can be found in the HRTEM image of the GQDs as seen in Fig. 2 d [17]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1e) displayed uniform atomic array and standard six-fold symmetry, indicating the single-crystal structure of W-GQDs. The statistical histogram displayed in Fig. 1f confirmed that the W-GQDs have a mean size of 2.65 nm and standard deviation of 0.6 nm by statistical analysis of over 200 particles. Overall, these results well-demonstrate the successful synthesis of GQDs with good dispersity and narrow size distribution.

The AFM topography image and a typical height profile of the W-GQDs are exhibited in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. We can see that the thickness of the obtained dots is below 1 nm, which is equal to 1–3 graphene layers. From TEM and AFM results, it is believed that typical GQDs were successfully synthesized rather than carbon quantum dots (CQDs), according to the nomenclature reported by Yang et al. that GQDs possess obvious graphene lattices and their horizontal size (generally <20 nm) is usually much larger than their height (generally <2.5 nm) [18]. As seen the Raman spectrum in Fig. 2c, the characteristic D peak at 1340 cm−1 and G peak at 1561 cm−1 can be clearly found, which are originated from the breathing mode of sp3 carbon rings and the bond stretching of sp2 carbon pairs, respectively [19,20]. The calculated peak intensity ratio of ID/IG is 1.02, and the relative low graphitization degree of W-GQDs is attributed to the doping of nitrogen and oxygen elements. The chemical structure analysis of W-GQDs was conducted by FT-IR, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. The broad absorption bands arisen from the stretching vibrations of O–H (phenolic/carboxyl) and N–H bond are observed at 3415 cm−1 and 3233 cm−1, separately. The weak bands located at 2967, 2921 and 2847 cm−1 are attributed to bending vibrations of –CH3/-CH2. The strong absorption bands at 1738 and 1614 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO (carboxylic) and CC (aromatic), respectively. The stretching vibration bands located at 1532 and 1378 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of CN bond and C–N in the structure of GQDs. The C–O–C and C–H bending vibrations were confirmed at 1127 and 1011 cm−1 [21,22]. Overall, the presence of CO and CN would redshift the emission peak of GQDs by increasing the conjugated π-domains.

The XPS survey spectrum is exhibited in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, there are three peaks centered at 284 eV for C1s, 399 eV for N1s and 532 eV for O1s, which is in line with the FT-IR results. The calculated atomic percentages of C, N and O in W-GQDs are 89.09%, 4.79%, and 6.12%, respectively. Fig. 3b shows the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, which can be divided into four peaks at 284.84, 285.59, 286.40, and 288.22 eV, representing the existence of CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively. Further more, the analyzed high resolution N1s peak confirmed the presence of pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N at 398.4, 399.20, 400.10, and 401.67 eV as shown in Fig. 3c. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) was also fitted well, and the CO (530.66 eV) and C–O (532.69 eV) have been found, which are originated from hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of TA molecules [23]. Based on the Raman, FT-IR and XPS results, we confirmed the formation of π-conjugated framework in inner structure and rich functional groups at the edge of W-GQDs. Thus, multi-peak emission of W-GQDs can be obtained. Besides, the high C content increases the crystallinity of product, which can help forming GQDs rather than carbon nanodots (CNDs). The introduction of nitrogen atoms in the structure of W-GQDs increased its high PLQY [10]. The structure of W-GQDs has been configured as seen in Fig. 3e.

The UV–vis spectrum of the W-GQDs solution is presented in Fig. 4 a. The absorption peak around 294 nm is arisen from the π-π* transition of CC bonds and another absorption peak centered at ∼447 nm is originated from n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds in W-GQDs particles, respectively [24–27]. In general, the absorption band from 200 to 350 nm is presented in almost all of reported GQDs, which should be responsible for the blue emission band from 400 nm to 480 nm (Band 1, Fig. 4b), when the W-GQDs was excited at 300 nm [28]. As a result, when excited from 420 nm to 500 nm, the GQDs emitted yellow-green light with a single peak centered at the wavelength of 558 nm (Band 2) as seen in Fig. 4b. We also observed that the W-GQDs showed typical excitation-independent PL behaviors, which might be due to the uniform microstructure and similar chemical states of the W-GQDs particles [29]. Besides, the intensity of Band 1 increased as excited from 280 nm to 300 nm, and decreased from 300 nm to 400 nm. For Band 2, the PL intensity monotonically decreased as the excitation wavelength varies from 280 nm to 500 nm with an interval of 20 nm. The trend is consistent with the result of excitation spectrum of W-GQDs as demonstrated in Fig. S1. Interestingly, we discovered that the obtained emission spectrum (Fig. S2) of W-GQDs in water has a single wide PL peak located around 458 nm, which is different from that in ethanol solution. It is because of the different polarity of water [30]. Since the solvents can mainly affect the surface functional groups, the result confirmed that the surface functional groups greatly affected the recombination dynamics of GQDs (surface-state and core-state).

Until now, despite the vague PL mechanism of GQDs, it is widely accepted that the PL behaviors are mainly attributed to the presence of core states (electronic conjugate configuration) and surface states (surface functional groups) [31,32]. In our case, we speculate that the PL behaviors of Band 1 and Band 2 maybe mainly produced from the surface states and core states, respectively: i) The oxygen-containing functional groups (especially for carboxyl and carbonyl groups) on the surface mainly originated from TA molecules and amino groups connected with the carbon backbone of W-GQDs particles prefer to form the emission centers of blue emission (Band 1) [33]; ii) The conjugated π-domains could be increased because of the formation of unsaturated oxygen/nitrogen-containing bonds and CC bonds in the intrinsic structure; iii) the π-conjugated framework is further enhanced due to the benzene ring in the 1,5-DAN precursors, which is tend to result in the core state emission (Band 2) [34]. To study the role of each precursor in the reaction, GQDs samples were also prepared using 1,5-DAN or TA as single carbon source, which was denoted as D-GQDs and T-GQDs, respectively. As shown in Figs. S3 and S4, the D-GQDs and T-GQDs exhibit only single and broad PL peak centered at 415 nm and 420 nm, respectively, which is evidently different from W-GQDs. It seems that both the benzene ring and oxygen-containing functional groups play a vital role in the forming process of W-GQDs, thereby leading to the generation of yellowish-green emission.

As seen the inset of Fig. 4b, the W-GQDs in ethanol clearly emitted bright white light under the excitation of 365 nm UV lamp, which should be resulted from the combination of core-state emission and surface-state emission. To further ensure its white emission, the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates was determined by calculating the PL spectra. The calculated chromaticity coordinates are (0.341, 0.381), (0.278, 0.258), (0.290, 0.299), (0.309, 0.343), (0.279, 0.293), (0.271, 0.286), and (0.298, 0.336) in Fig. 4c, corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380 and 400 nm, respectively. All of these CIE coordinates are apparently located within the gamut of white luminescence in the chromaticity diagram. It is in line with the above results. Besides, to evaluate the exciton kinetics, transient PL spectroscopy was used to measure the PL decay, and the obtained decay curves were fitted well by a bi-exponential decay function as seen in Fig. 4d. The calculated average exciton lifetime of W-GQDs collected at 435 nm and 558 nm is about 7.67 ns and 9.40 ns (Table S1), respectively. It is clear that the two emissions were generated from different states. Similar result has been reported in literature [35]. The stability of W-GQDs in ethanol exposed under daylight and UV light has been evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. 4e–f, the PL intensity of the W-GQDs has decreased by maximum of 6% only for 30 days under ambient condition. Even being exposed for 12 h under UV light, the sample still has 92% of the initial PL intensity, showing good stablility against light bleaching. It is comparable to that of previously reported outstanding white-light-emissive GQDs [26,36,37]. In addition, the thermal stability of W-GQDs was also investigated. As seen in Fig. S5, the W-GQDs have comparable PL intensity in the temperature range of 5 °C to 60 °C, indicating their good thermal stability. These results confirm the good stability of W-GQDs, which is significant for their applications.

The obtained W-GQDs with good stability could emit photons in the whole visible range from 380 to 700 nm. Therefore, it is promising for white-light luminescence materials application as the active material. Before the fabrication of luminous polymer films, the quantum yield (QY) of W-GQDs was measured to be 28.68%, among the best values of direct-synthesized white-light-emissive GQDs [5,16,37–40]. More details can be found in Table S2. Furthermore, PVA and epoxy resin were chosen as the host materials to fabricate the white-light-emitting polymer with high transparency and good stability. In that, the existence of long-chain polymer enlarges the distance among the GQDs particles, hindering the fluorescence quenching. The photographs of W-GQDs/PVA film exposed under illumination of daylight and 365 nm UV are displayed in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively. In the meantime, Fig. 5c exhibits bright white light and good flexibility of W-GQDs/PVA film composite, which is acquired through mixing W-GQDs with PVA and drying in a petri dish. The QY of W-GQDs/PVA film was measured to be 9.32%, among the best values of PVA-based devices [16,41]. Moreover, the W-GQDs/epoxy resin film with the pattern of “NCU” letters was prepared by the similar technique, except the moulds were used for cooling the mixture GQDs solution. As seen in Fig. 5d and e, the obtained W-GQDs/epoxy resin film shows high transparency and bright white luminescence under the irradiation of 365 nm laser. Besides, the CRI value of W-GQDs was measured to be 80.2, signifying that it is promising for lighting application. These results well demonstrated the high quality of our synthesized GQDs. Overall, our prepared luminescent films can be potentially applied in solid-state lighting device. The schematic illustration of preparing solid luminescent films is shown in Scheme 2 as below.

In brief, the white-light emissive GQDs have been synthesized in an one-pot reaction using 1,5-DAN and TA as the carbon sources. The as-prepared W-GQDs have two emission bands in the blue region and yellow-green region, respectively, arising from the surface-state and core-state. Remarkably, the W-GQDs exhibited a PLQY as high as 28.68%, and good photo-stability under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp and daylight. As a matter of fact, the PLQY of the prepared white luminescence W-GQDs/PVA film based on W-GQDs reached 9.32%. Overall, we developed a facile way to synthesize single-component white-light GQDs with high PLQY, which is promising for WLED application.

Z.L.: performed the experiments, analyzed the data, wrote the paper, T. L.: conceived and designed the experiments, wrote the paper, P.P.H.: analyzed the data, X.J.J.: analyzed the data, M.C.L.: analyzed the data, Q.Y.Z.: analyzed the data, P.L.: analyzed the data, Z.L.Z: analyzed the data, S.D.: analyzed the data, G.-P.D.: conceived and designed the experiments, wrote the paper, analyzed the data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: G.-P.D acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 52062033 and 51762032) for financial support of this research. L. T. is grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 61904075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2022.112368.
